coredata.dc:title: Nonenzymatic interference free nano-molar range detection of L-tyrosine in human serum and urine samples using carbon dots doped cerium titanate modified glassy carbon electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Shobana, Babu
Gayathri, Loganathan
Sathish Kumar, Ponnaiah
Prakash, Periakaruppan
coredata.dc:description: Normally wound healing is much slower in diabetic patients. Therefore it leaves them susceptible to ulceration and subsequent infection. The concentration of L-Tyrosine (Tyr), a non-essential amino acid is found to be significantly elevated in serum and urine samples of patients with infected ulcers. In situ monitoring and early detection of Tyr levels is therefore pivotal in the timely diagnosis of infections in ulcers of diabetics. Tyr, unlike other inflammatory biomarkers, is more specific for wound related infections and is a better candidate as biomarker which necessitates researchers to design novel biosensors for the detection of infections. With this intent we have devised a sensitive biosensor system that uses a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with the synthesized cerium titanate oxide doped carbon dots (CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE) for sensing Tyr in biological samples. UV–Vis. spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy, XRD technique, FE-SEM, Mapping, HR-TEM, EDX and XPS analyses were used to characterize the synthesized CeTi2O6/CDs. The biosensor system was optimized after studying the effect of variables such as loading of the CeTi2O6/CDs nanocomposite, concentration of Tyr and pH of the solution. The lower detection limit of Tyr was found to be 26 nM under ideal settings where the linear variation between the oxidation peak current of Tyr and its concentration ranging from 0.01 to 230 µM. The CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE was found to be a specific, reliable and highly sensitive system for quantification of Tyr in biological samples, which paves way for early detection of onset of infection thereby preventing complications associated with diabetic foot ulcers like gangrene and sepsis that in turn leads to limb amputation.

head.ce:title: Nonenzymatic interference free nano-molar range detection of L-tyrosine in human serum and urine samples using carbon dots doped cerium titanate modified glassy carbon electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: PG & Research Department of Chemistry, Thiagarajar College, Affiliated to Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai-09, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: PG & Research Department of Chemistry, Thiagarajar College, Affiliated to Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai-09, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nano-molar range detection of L-tyrosine using carbon dots doped cerium titanate modified glassy carbon electrode.

Normally wound healing is much slower in diabetic patients. Therefore it leaves them susceptible to ulceration and subsequent infection. The concentration of L-Tyrosine (Tyr), a non-essential amino acid is found to be significantly elevated in serum and urine samples of patients with infected ulcers. In situ monitoring and early detection of Tyr levels is therefore pivotal in the timely diagnosis of infections in ulcers of diabetics. Tyr, unlike other inflammatory biomarkers, is more specific for wound related infections and is a better candidate as biomarker which necessitates researchers to design novel biosensors for the detection of infections. With this intent we have devised a sensitive biosensor system that uses a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with the synthesized cerium titanate oxide doped carbon dots (CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE) for sensing Tyr in biological samples. UV–Vis. spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy, XRD technique, FE-SEM, Mapping, HR-TEM, EDX and XPS analyses were used to characterize the synthesized CeTi2O6/CDs. The biosensor system was optimized after studying the effect of variables such as loading of the CeTi2O6/CDs nanocomposite, concentration of Tyr and pH of the solution. The lower detection limit of Tyr was found to be 26 nM under ideal settings where the linear variation between the oxidation peak current of Tyr and its concentration ranging from 0.01 to 230 µM. The CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE was found to be a specific, reliable and highly sensitive system for quantification of Tyr in biological samples, which paves way for early detection of onset of infection thereby preventing complications associated with diabetic foot ulcers like gangrene and sepsis that in turn leads to limb amputation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids are enantiomer compounds that are the building blocks of proteins. Since proteins are one of the key biomolecules in the mechanics of any living organism, the amino acids they constitute gain clinical significance as biomarkers of several diseases. Amino acids play crucial roles as neurotransmitters and neuromodulators. Being key players at various levels in metabolism they are implicated in various age related diseases, especially neurodegenerative conditions like Alzheimer disease, Parkinson’s disease and Schizophrenia [1–3]. The usage of amino acid profile of a patient as a potential biomarker in disease diagnosis is a new and emerging area of systems biology known as Metabolomic Profiling [4–5]. L- Tyrosine (Tyr) is a nonessential amino acid, which can be synthesized in the body when its dietary intake is insufficient. Tyr is synthesized from phenylalanine by the action of the enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase. The role of Tyr in human physiology can hardly be underestimated. At least 3.2% of most of the proteins comprise Tyr residues [6]. Tyr is an inevitable precursor in the synthesis of catecholamines including norepinephrine, l-dopamine, dopamine, epinephrine, which are mono-amine neurotransmitters that operate the body’s autonomic nervous system [7–8]. Tyr is also a precursor in the synthesis of thyroxine hormone as well as melanin pigment the deficiency of which leads to hypothyroidism and albinism respectively [9–10]. Inborn errors in metabolism of Tyr also lead to several disorders such as hereditary tyrosinaemia (Type I, Type II and Type III), and hawkinsinuria and alkaptonuria characterized by progressive liver disease and renal tubular dysfunction, keratitis, mental retardation and metabolic acidosis [11]. Normally, the concentration of Tyr in healthy individuals ranges from 30 to 120 μM [12]. Abnormal levels of Tyr are associated with neurodegenerative diseases, depression and mood disorders. Over usage of Tyr as a drug can cause side effects such as headache, nausea, fatigue, joint pain and heartburn [13–16].

The association of Tyr in several diseases makes it a useful biomarker in diagnosis. The aforesaid reasons warrant the necessity to devise a reliable and rapid method for estimation of Tyr in biological samples. The methods available for the quantification of amino acids in biological samples include amino acid analyzer, capillary electrophoresis, fluorescence detection, gas chromatography mass spectrometry (GC–MS), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), luminescence, liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC- MS), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR). Among these, HPLC, LC–MS and GC–MS are commonly used and sensitive among others [17–18]. HPLC involves high cost columns and many organic solvents. Luminescence detection is generally time consuming and requires annealing and recalibration after each use for accuracy. Moreover, each of these methods requires skilled technicians, expensive equipment and is relatively laborious and time consuming [19]. A simple, reliable, sensitive and easy to use device that can be used in-situ clinical settings would go a long way in timely diagnosis and treatment of several diseases, which is where electrochemical nano biosensors can be an efficient alternative and such is the scope of the present study.

Electrochemical methods of quantification have several advantages of over existing methods such as ease of operation, cost effectiveness, rapid response and high sensitivity [20]. Several studies have been carried out in fabrication of suitable nanomaterials for biosensor systems. Examples include cupric oxide doped β-cyclodextrin nanocomposites for L-Tyr detection by amperometric i-t method [8], tetraruthenated cobalt(II) porphyrin intercalated into a smectite clay in determination of acetaminophen and tyrosine by square wave voltammograms [9], CuO/Cu2O nanoparticles and multiwalled carbon nanotubes nanocomposites for the linear sweep voltammetric detection of Tyr [10], graphene oxide and multiwalled carbon nanotubes to determine Tyr [13], graphene oxide doped acetylene black for the sensitive sensing of Tyr [14] and electrochemical detection of Tyr using quantum dots and β- cyclodextrins [1]. Using bare electrodes in electrochemical detection of metabolites in biological samples has its own challenges. Presence of other biomolecules in the samples that have the similar oxidation potential as the target molecule can interfere with the accuracy of results. The deposition of oxidation products on the surface of bare electrodes can reduce the reusability and reproducibility of the sensor. To overcome these shortcomings, surface modification of electrodes is usually carried out. The materials used for surface modification increase selectivity and sensitivity of the biosensor. The surface modification facilitates better conduction of electrons between target and electrode that induces the fast oxidation or reduction of target resulting in better peak separation between analytes. Overall sensitivity, reproducibility and electrochemical response are better in surface modified electrodes of biosensors [21].

Among the various choices of compounds available, the rare earth titanates have been chosen in the present study. Titanates are ideal choices in biosensor systems because of sustainability, high chemical and thermal stability and biocompatibility with large active surface area [22–23]. Titanates have unique crystal structure and are stable in physiological fluids making them good candidate for use in biomolecule detection [24]. Moreover, nanostructured titanates have other inherent advantages such as low cost, low toxicity, high photocatalytic and electrocatalytic activity. These in combination with an exceptionally large surface area and photo corrosion resistance make nano titanates an irresistible option for biosensor designing [25]. Among titanate compounds, cerium titanates (Ce2TiO5, CeTi2O6, Ce2Ti2O7, Ce4Ti9O24, CeTiO4, and CeTi2O6) are more beneficial for biosensor fabrication owing to their stability, surface oxygen vacancies and electronic properties [26–27]. Particularly, nano-CeTi2O6 is one of the most important nanomaterials, which attracts more research interest in the recent past owing to its applications in oxidation catalysis, photocatalysis, gas sensing and fuel cells [28–29]. However, the downside to CeTi2O6 nanomaterial is its semiconducting properties which compromise their electrical conductivity to a certain extent. This can be overcome by compositing this nanotitanate with nanocarbon materials. Carbonaceous materials exhibit favorable properties such as good electric conductivity, high power density and large surface area at a low cost [30–34]. On the other hand, carbonaceous materials do exhibit inadequacies such as high charging currents and electrochemical interferences. When titanates and carbon nanomaterials are composited, they nullify each other’s shortcomings thereby make a winning combination in fabrication of suitable surface modification agents for biosensors [35]. Among carbon based nanomaterials, Carbon Dots (CDs) less than 10 nm in size possess added advantages of biocompatibility, low toxicity, chemical inertness, electronic conductivity, porosity with high chemical stability and surface functionalization properties. These attributes make them ideal candidates for a variety of applications including sensors, super capacitors, bio imagining systems and drug delivery [36–38].

In the present study, a novel CeTi2O6/CDs based electrode material has been designed for electrochemicals detection of Tyr through voltammetric method. The combination of CeTi2O6 and CDs has been proven to exhibit high electrocatalytic activity towards sensing of Tyr. The voltammetric detection of Tyr using CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE shows better results. The synthesized CeTi2O6/CDs nanocomposite was characterized by XRD, HR-TEM, UV–Vis., FT-IR, EDAX, FE-SEM, Mapping and XPS. The designed biosensor was validated by testing the levels of Tyr in human blood and urine samples successfully.

Cerium(III) nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O), citric acid (CA), ethanol, ascorbic acid, L-methionine, L-leucine, L-histidine, L-arginine, L-threonine, L-tyrosine, L-valine, NaOH and titanium isopropoxide were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. Aqueous solution of sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) and dibasic anhydrous sodium phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) were used to prepare phosphate buffer (PB). The pH was regulated using dilute HCl and NaOH. The PB with 0.05 M and pH 6.0 was used as an electrolyte throughout the electrochemical experiments. Deionized water (DIW) was used for prepare all the solutions. The details regarding the instrumentation are given in electronic supplementary materials (ESM) (Table S1).

The CDs were prepared from CA as reported elsewhere [31,36]. The CeTi2O6/CDs powder was prepared via sol-gel technique. Titanium isopropoxide (1.70 mL) was mixed with 16.0 mL of ethanol with constant stirring. To this solution was then added 0.9304 g of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 16.0 mL DIW and 16.0 mL of acetic acid. The mixture was stirred for an hour. Then 3 mL of CDs thoroughly dispersed in DIW was added drop wise under steady magnetic stirring at room temperature. It was subjected to sonication for 2 h and the gel formed was dried at 60 °C overnight which in a muffle furnace was calcined at 800 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The sol-gel synthesis protocol for CeTi2O6/CDs and its application for electrochemical detection is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of glassy carbon electrode modified with CeTi2O6/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Before the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified, it was first cleaned with alumina silica powder (0.05 µm) and then washed with ethanol and DIW. The as-prepared CeTi2O6/CDs (2.0 mg) in 1.0 mL of DIW by sonicating for 20 min. Then about 6 µL (as optimized) of completely dispersed CeTi2O6/CDs was drop-casted onto the already-cleaned GCE and dried a hot air oven. The electrode thus modified with CeTi2O6/CDs is termed as CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE and used in all electrochemical measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Tyr by the CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The fabricated working electrode based on CeTi2O6/CDs modified GCE, platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode, and Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as the reference electrode and aqueous 0.05 M PB (pH – 6.0) were used as the electrolyte. To complete this study, the selective Try was DIW and a series of solutions were prepared with different concentrations (0.01–130 µM) and applied to electrochemical analysis (CV and DPV) using a three-electrode cell (Model 660E, Austin, TX, USA) under nitrogen atmosphere at room temperature. A calibration curve obtained from the linear relation of current versus concentration was used to evaluate the analytical parameters of the sensor such as LOD, LOQ and sensitivity. The efficiency of the proposed sensor was estimated in term of response time and reliability from the reproducibility performance.

The synthesized CDs show strong photosensitive absorption in the UV region and the absorption peak is observed at 318 nm resulting from π-π* transition as shown in Fig. S1a (curve – a). Depending on defects in the structure, roughness of the surface, size of the grains, strain in lattice planes and deficiency of oxygen, an absorption edge in the visible region is exposed by pure CeTi2O6 (curve – b) [39]. An increased visible light absorbance for CeTi2O6/CDs (curve – c) indicates the compositing of CDs over CeTi2O6; the CDs are embedded on the surface of CeTi2O6 via simple physisorption, electrostatic binding or charge transfer interactions [38].

The FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. S1b (curve – a)) shows a broad band in the range, 3100–3900 cm−1, which is due to stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H. The characteristic bands at 2950–2850 cm−1 and 1620–1750 cm−1 correspond to the C–H and C = C vibrations respectively. The peaks at 1340–1560 cm−1 and 1140 cm−1 are allotted to the C–H, N–H, C–O, C–O–C bending vibrations [40]. The O–H and C–H stretching vibrations exhibit the characteristic peaks at 3480 and 2868–2978 cm−1 for pure CeTi2O6 (curve – b). The band observed at around 2348 and 2367 cm−1 is pertaining to CO2 and Ce–O vibration shows a peak at 508 cm−1. Another band observed at about 1752 cm−1 corresponds to water Ti–OH bending modes. A conspicuous peak at 1367 cm−1 is related to Ti-O modes. Further, an absorption band at 512 cm−1 is due to the Ti–O vibration and the band at 486 cm−1 originates from Ti–O–C stretching [41]. Careful observation of curve – c of the FT-IR spectrum of CeTi2O6/CDs composite infers that the position and intensity of the peaks are well affected upon compositing of CDs and CeTi2O6 notwithstanding the spectra of CeTi2O6 and CDs are similar. From all these characteristic peaks in the spectrum of the nanocomposite, it is worth mentioning here that the CDs are assembled successfully onto the CeTi2O6 [35].

The particle nature, purity of phase and lattice parameters of as-synthesized CDs, CeTi2O6 and CeTi2O6/CDs nanocomposite were studied using X-ray diffraction technique. The XRD pattern for CDs is presented in Fig. S1c (curve – a). It shows a broader (002) peak at 24° belonging to the turbostratic phase of CDs (JCPDS card no. 79–1469). The pattern for pristine CeTi2O6 is shown in Fig. S1c (curve – b) which indicates that the substance crystallizes into monoclinic system with brannerite structure. The cubic CeO2 shows a weak diffraction peak at 2θ = 56.3° (JCPDS 08-0291) [42–43]. The XRD pattern similar to that of CeTi2O6 phase is observed for the composite material CeTi2O6/CDs (curve c) with slight shift towards higher angles, which clearly indicates that the CDs are attached on the supporting matrix, CeTi2O6 as exposed in Fig. S1c. These results from XRD study confirm the successful compositing of CeTi2O6 and CDs. Moreover the average particle size of the nanocomposite, CeTi2O6/CDs was determined from the lattice parameters at most prominent peak (113) of CeTi2O6/CDs using Debye − Scherrer equation: D = Kλ/β Cos (θ), where D is the grain particle size (nm), K is Scherrer constant (0.9), λ is the wavelength of X-ray (0.154 nm) and β is the full-width half-maximum. The average particle size of the nanocomposite, CeTi2O6/CDs from (113) peak of XRD by using the above equation is found to be 20.53 nm.

FESEM image of CeTi2O6/CDs electrocatalyst (Fig. S2(a and b)) shows that CDs exist with CeTi2O6 materials and there is a giant amount of tiny CDs distributed over CeTi2O6 microspheres. The purity of the samples was checked by elemental mapping analysis, which shows that carbon (C), cerium (Ce), titanium (Ti), and oxygen (O) coexist in abundance (Fig.S2(c – f)). HR-TEM was used to explore the surface morphology of CeTi2O6/CDs. The images are shown in Fig. 1 (a – c). The as-synthesized CDs are spherical, which are closely attached with CeTi2O6 as shown in Fig. 1a. The black colored particles with size less than 10 nm correspond to carbon dots (CDs) as shown in the Fig. S3(a and b), which distinguishes the CD spheres from CeTi2O6 spheres, the size of which is relatively higher. Fig. 1(b and c) demonstrates that CDs particles are entrenched over CeTi2O6 nanostructure. The highly magnified HR-TEM images as shown in Fig. 1(b and c) provide a strong evidence for the intercalation of CDs over CeTi2O6. Furthermore, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 1d) of the nanocomposite shows concentric diffraction rings, which indicate that the nanocomposite, CeTi2O6/CDs is crystalline in nature.

In addition, EDAX analysis (Fig. 1e) was carried out to examine the composition of Ce, Ti, O and C in the as synthesized CeTi2O6/CDs. The tabulated results offer the elemental composition in units of both weight present (Ce: 27.07, Ti: 39.71, O: 3.16 and C: 2.98) and atomic present (Ce: 32.85, Ti: 16.47, O: 11.47 and C: 14.43) in the inspection field as shown in Fig. 1e (inset).

In XPS studies, the existing elements of CeTi2O6/CDs were examined for their oxidation states and binding energies. According to the CeTi2O6/CDs electrocatalyst XPS survey (Fig. 2 a), there was a major peak for C, Ti, Ce, and O with binding energies ranging from 284.7, 458.7 to 883.2 eV, and 530.5 eV. As for Ce 3d elements (Fig. 2b), Ce3+ ions are responsible for peaks observed at 882.4 and 885.6 eV, while Ce4+ ions are responsible for peaks observed at 901.1, 904.7, and 916.6 eV [44].

In Fig. 2c, the O 1 s spectrum is fitted into two peaks with observed at 529.4, and 531.8 eV, which corresponds to the Ti-O bond and the OH group on surface, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2d, the two distinct peaks observed at 458.1 and 463.9 eV correspond to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 spins of the CeTi2O6. Fig. 2e shows the deconvoluted C1s spectrum was found to have two peaks at 284.7 and 288.2 eV, which are associated with C-C bonds of surface adsorbed carbon and hybridized carbon in C = N of CDs matrix [45–46]. On the basis of these results, it can be concluded that there is a composite between CeTi2O6 and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of CeTi2O6/CDs modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The electrode-electrolyte interfacial resistance has been facilitated by using electron impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. By using the EIS technique, the electron charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the as-prepared nanocomposite was visualized and the corresponding Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. S4a. To perform the EIS analysis, 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl was used. The potential was set to 10 mV and the frequency ranged from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. As shown by the EIS curve, the semicircular diameter of CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE, CeTi2O6/GCE CDs/GCE and bare GCE are Rct = 298.7, 396.6, 520.1, and 708.9 Ω. It is confirmed in these results that CeTi2O6 and CDs enhance electron transport, which is also consistent with CV results. Since the unmodified GCE lacks electron transport abilities, it has a larger semicircle with a larger diameter than CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE. The modified electrode CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE performed excellent electrocatalytic activity towards the detection of Tyr due to its good electron transfer and large surface area.

The electrochemical properties of bare GCE, CDs/GCE, CeTi2O6/GCE and CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE were investigated using CVs in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate of 20 mVs−1. In Fig. S4b sharp and well-defined redox peak was observed at the lower positive and negative potential separation of the CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE with ΔEp = 97 mV. Based on the observed results, CeTi2O6/CDs were effectively coated on GCE surfaces. This result indicates enhanced electrochemical activity with a large electroactive surface area in comparison to other unmodified and modified GCE [4]. Also to evaluate the electrochemically active surface area (ECAS) of CeTi2O6/CDs modified GCE in an electrolyte containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl at different scan rates from 20 to 200 mV/s, as shown in Fig. S4c. The good increment in both anodic and cathodic peak currents was observed with increasing scan rates. The resultant peak currents were plotted with redox peak current vs. square root of scan rate, which showed a good linear relationship (Fig. S4d). Randles-Sevick equation was used to estimate the ECASs of modified bare GCE, CDs/GCE, CeTi2O6/GCE and CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE [47],

where, A is the surface area (cm2), C is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (mol/cm3), n is the number of involved electrons, υ is the scan rate (Vs −1) and ip is the redox peak current. According to the calculation, the electroactive surface area of modified CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE is 0.072 cm2 respectively. This resultant electroactive surface of CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE, is very helpful to promote the electrochemical performance towards Tyr sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of drop casting load of CeTi2O6/CDs over GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The possible way of CeTi2O6 interacting with CDs is via π-π interaction and holds higher electrocatalytic activity [31]. Thus the combination of these two materials shows better performance in detecting the analyte. Here the loading amount of electrode modifier is an important parameter which affects the electrochemical response of modified GCE. Hence, in the presence of 70 µM concentration of Tyr at 50 mV/s (0.05 M PB (pH – 6.0)), CV experiments were made at different load of CeTi2O6/CDs suspension that was drop casted on the GCE. The corresponding CV response curves are shown in Fig. S5a where the 12 µg loading of CeTi2O6/CDs attains the maximum anodic current (Ipa = 34.92 µA) compared to 4 µg, 8 µg and 16 µg and the anodic peak current corresponding to these loadings are 30.81 µA, 32.34 µA and 33.63 µA respectively. The anodic current that drops at 4 and 8 µg is due to the thin surface area of CeTi2O6/CDs suspension, which has no or little part in the movement of electrons. On the other hand, increase of more amounts of CeTi2O6/CDs suspension (16 µg) results in the accumulation of nanocomposite material, which impedes with the electron transfer kinetics [48]. Therefore 12 µg of CeTi2O6/CDs suspension was found to be optimum drop cast loading and drop casted unvaryingly in all the electrochemical investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on Tyr detection using CeTi2O6/CDs modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The pH plays a vital role in determining the peak current and peak potential. Moreover the charge on the modified electrode surface can be affected by the pH of the solution, which stimulates different interactions between the substance to be detected and CeTi2O6/CDs. Therefore the CV study was carried out in different pH ranging from 2.0 to 8.0 in the presence of 70 µM concentration of Tyr at 50 mV/s as shown in Fig. S5b. It was observed that with increase of pH from 2.0 to 8.0, the oxidative peak current increased and it was maximum at pH 6.0, which decreased after this pH. It should be noted here that the increase of pH causes the carboxylic groups of Tyr to load negatively and these negative groups interact more efficiently with the positive charge of the electrode that is modified with nanocomposite material. Thus the affinity for the sensor toward the analyte increases [48]. On the other hand, the protons taking part in the electrode reactions result in decrease of peak current with the increase of pH of the solution. Hence pH 6.0 was fixed optimally for the sensing of Tyr in all electrochemical experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electro-oxidation reaction of Tyr at modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electro-oxidation behavior of Tyr at different modified and unmodified electrodes namely CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE (Fig. S5c (red color)) CeTi2O6/GCE (blue color), CDs/GCE (pink color) and bare GCE (black color) was studied in the presence of 70 µM concentration of Tyr at 50 mV/s in the potential range of +0.3 V to +1.0 V with optimized drop casting load of nanocomposite and pH. The Ipa for CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE is found to be 38.04 µA which is greater than that obtained for bare GCE (12.11 µA), CDs/GCE (21.60 µA) and CeTi2O6/GCE (29.68 µA). While adding Tyr to CDs, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in the surface of the CDs interact with the hydroxyl amide and carboxyl groups present in the Tyr moiety through hydrogen bonding interaction. In addition, π-π interaction from the aromatic stack of CDs and Tyr is also possible [49]. Hence the electrocatalytic activity of the bare GCE is very much improved when it is modified with CeTi2O6/CDs, with faster electron transfer kinetics resulting from higher electronic conductivity and manifold redox reactions [50]. From these results, it is evident that the electrode modification with the nanocomposite, CeTi2O6/CDs could be better used for the electrochemical detection of Tyr. When the electron loss of L-Tyr is electrochemically determined in the electron transfer reaction, the CeTi2O6/CDs modified GCE is converted to an acceptor, which gives the intermediate product para-hydroxyphenylpyruvate or 3,4-dihydroxy -L-phenylalanine, which is a dimer and loses another electron to form the homogentisic acid (S)-2-amino-3-(4-oxocyclohexa-2,5-dien-1-ylidene)propanoic acid an oxidized product [51]. In view of the observed results, the electron transfer mechanism for the oxidation of L-Tyr is proposed, which involves CeTi2O6/CDs in an equal ratio between protons and electrons as illustrated in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate and Tyr concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The rate of charge transfer and electron transfer was determined using CV in the presence of 20 µM concentration of Tyr and the scan rate varying from 10 to 150 mV/s. From Fig. 3 a, it can be seen that there is an increasing trend of peak current value. The linear dependence plot of scan rate vs. current as shown in Fig. 3b holds a linear regression equation and correlation coefficient (Ipa = 0. 0952 mV/s; R2 = 0.9921). Similarly Fig. 3c exposes the plot of square root of sweep rate (mV/s)1/2 vs. current (μA) wherein the linear equation with correlation coefficient offer Ipa = 1.3630 (mV/s)1/2 and R2 = 0.9969. The correlation coefficient values equaling almost unity implies the electro redox behavior of Tyr at GCE modified with CeTi2O6/CDs, undergoes an adsorption-controlled process than that of the diffusion-controlled process respectively [52]. Besides the electron transfer, the kinetic rate was studied with the linear calibration plot between anodic potential (Epa) vs. log scan rate (log (mV/s) as shown in Fig. 3d. The regression equation and correlation coefficient are given as Ipa = 0. 5734 (mV/s) − 0.0584 and R2 = 0.9985. The correlation coefficient for electro-oxidation peaks is almost equal to 1, which further confirms the diffusion-controlled reaction [53]. The catalytic diffusion rate constant of Tyr oxidation on CeTi2O6/CDs were shown by CV method in Fig. S6.

The concentration effect of Tyr was studied by changing the concentration of it from 10 to 130 µM and the results obtained are shown in Fig. 4 a. The electro-oxidation peak current is found to increase upon increasing the concentration of Tyr. The linear relation of the electro-oxidation performance of the modified electrode with increase in concentration of Tyr is shown in Fig. 4b with the correlation coefficient value of R2 = 0.9983, which is almost equal to 1 signifying the higher electrocatalytic activity of the proposed sensor. This indicates that the electrocatalytic performance of the modified electrode, CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE follows the first order kinetics process during Tyr detection [10].

The critical parameters such as limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ) and the sensitivity of modified electrode were realized using DPV technique by varying the Tyr concentration from 0.01 to 230 μM in 0.05 M PB (pH 6.0). The anodic peak current is found to increase with an increase in concentration of Tyr as shown in Fig. 4c. The oxidation peak current of Tyr plotted against the concentration of Tyr ranging 0.01 to 230 µM as shown in Fig. 4d exhibits a linearity. The regression equation is given as, y = 0.2367x + 0.7204 and the R2 = 0.9973. The slope obtained for the lower concentration was used to determine the LOD (3 σ/S), LOQ (10 σ/Sb) and sensitivity (slope/working surface area) of the GCE modified with CeTi2O6/CDs, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank, S and Sb denote the slope obtained from the calibration plot. Upon deducing LOD, LOQ and sensitivity using the above relation, the values are found to be 0.026 µM, 0.086 µM, and 2.817 μA μM−1 cm−2 respectively. These results indicate that the proposed sensor involving CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE shows better electrochemical sensing performance than the other modified electrodes (Table 1 ) reported in the literature vis-à-vis electrochemical detection of Tyr [54–65].

The important parameters such as selectivity, stability and reproducibility are very important contributing factors for practical applications of any sensing elements. Hence the ability of the proposed sensor, CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE to selectively sense the analyte, Tyr (120 µM) in the presence of various similar interferences was studied using DPV. The response of the electrode material to the three fold higher concentrations of interfering substrates including L-histidine, L-methionine, L-leucine, L-arginine, L-threonine, L-valine, ascorbic acid, 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-Htm) and tryptophan (trp) is shown in Fig. S7a. From the figure, it can be seen that the CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE responds selectively to Tyr and none of the signals of other groups interferes with the detection of Tyr. Fig. S7a indicates that the present system of detection of Tyr by CeTi2O6/CDs is interferents free. The connection between CeTi2O6 and CDs is mainly attributed to physisorption, electrostatic binding or charge transfer interactions originating from surface groups (–COOH, –OH) from CDs and CeTi2O6.

The selectivity of CeTi2O6/CDs towards Tyr is mainly due to the hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups, amide and carboxyl groups of Tyr and hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of CDs besides π-π interaction of the aromatic stack of CDs with Tyr [49]. The proposed sensor shows a tremendous selectivity towards Tyr in an environment of interfering class of similar substrates which will otherwise normally be influencing the detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, reproducibility and storage stability of CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Consecutive measurements (N = 15) were performed to examine the repeatability, reproducibility and stability of CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE in the presence of Tyr (70 µM) in 0.05 M PB (pH 6.0). The plot of anodic current acquired for 15 measurements is shown in Fig. S7b. It is seen from the figure that the oxidation current does not fluctuate much from the starting current. The relative standard deviation is calculated to be 0.98% which exposes the better repeatability characteristic of CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE. Similarly the reproducibility experiments were performed with three different individual GCE modified with an optimized load of CeTi2O6/CDs (Fig. S7c). The anodic current for these three electrodes varies with just ±5 % from the original current. Moreover the observation that the anodic current and potential of the individual electrodes are similar indicates better reproducibility of the proposed sensor. The DPV response deteriorated slightly after the initial cycle, with a 4.63% decrease in the observed signal. Furthermore, long-term stability of the electrode was also evaluated by measuring the electrochemical performance of CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE nanocomposite at different storage periods was tested by preserving the electrode for 5 weeks at room temperature (Fig. S7d). The CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE showed 96.6% storage stability (RSD = 4.83%) for the electro-oxidation of Tyr.

The practical viability of the fabricated sensor was evaluated in fresh human serum and first urine samples collected in the morning from three healthy adults. The separation of serum was done by centrifuging at 2800 rpm. 2.0 mL of per chloride acid (5.0 % v/v) was added to 2.0 mL of serum sample, which was then vortexed for 5 min. The proteins settled were separated by centrifuging at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant layer was filtered through a 0.45 mm Millipore filter paper and was made up to 25.0 mL using 0.1 M PB of pH 6.0. 10.0 mL of each urine sample was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant liquid was filtered using a 0.45 μm polypropylene syringe, which was then diluted 10 times using PB of pH 6.0 [13]. Fig. S8a and b shows the DPV responses for the serum and urine samples contaminated with 0.1 μM Tyr. The standard addition method was employed in the real sample analysis to eliminate the matrix effects, and the standard addition plots for the serum and urine samples are shown in Fig. S8c and d. The extrapolated intercepts with the x-axis revealed the unknown Tyr concentration in the real samples. The recovery results were calculated based on the standard addition method were estimated as: Recovery (%)=(1×10−6 mol/L Tyr found/1×10−6 mol/L Tyr added) × 100 and are listed in Table 2 . From the real sample investigation, it is concluded that the fabricated CeTi2O6/CDs/GCE sensor displays excellent recovery results (98.9–101.8 %) and the device can be applied with more accuracy and precision.

In summary, the nanocomposite of cerium titanate and carbon dots (CeTi2O6/CDs) was successfully synthesized via a simple sol-gel technique. The electrochemical properties of the glassy carbon electrode modified with the synthesized CeTi2O6/CDs nanocomposite were explored for Tyr detection using CV and DPV techniques. The combined electrocatalytic behavior of CeTi2O6 and CDs facilitated lower LOD, LOQ and higher sensitivity, which were found to be 26 nM, 86 nM, and 2.817 μA μM−1 cm−2 respectively. A comparison indicated that this sensing system performed better than the previously reported modified electrodes. A good repeatability, reproducibility and stability of reported sensor showed that the device could be used for the detection of Tyr in serum and urine samples with much accuracy and precision. The proposed biosensor system certainly provides scope for amino acid profiling of patients and asymptomatic individuals in clinical settings for timely diagnosis of amino acid associated disorders.

Babu Shobana: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Loganathan Gayathri: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Ponnaiah Sathish Kumar: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Periakaruppan Prakash: Conceptualization, Supervision, Investigation, Visualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.
